content.text:
# Keybindings

All keyboard shortcuts can be customized via `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`. Each action can be bound to one or more keys.

The config file uses the same namespaced keybinding ids that pi uses internally and that extension authors use in `keyHint()` and injected `keybindings` managers.

Older configs using pre-namespaced ids such as `cursorUp` or `expandTools` are migrated automatically to the namespaced ids on startup.

After editing `keybindings.json`, run `/reload` in pi to apply the changes without restarting the session.

## Key Format

`modifier+key` where modifiers are `ctrl`, `shift`, `alt` (combinable) and keys are:

- **Letters:** `a-z`
- **Digits:** `0-9`
- **Special:** `escape`, `esc`, `enter`, `return`, `tab`, `space`, `backspace`, `delete`, `insert`, `clear`, `home`, `end`, `pageUp`, `pageDown`, `up`, `down`, `left`, `right`
- **Function:** `f1`-`f12`
- **Symbols:** `` ` ``, `-`, `=`, `[`, `]`, `\`, `;`, `'`, `,`, `.`, `/`, `!`, `@`, `#`, `$`, `%`, `^`, `&`, `*`, `(`, `)`, `_`, `+`, `|`, `~`, `{`, `}`, `:`, `<`, `>`, `?`

Modifier combinations: `ctrl+shift+x`, `alt+ctrl+x`, `ctrl+shift+alt+x`, `ctrl+1`, etc.

## All Actions

### TUI Editor Cursor Movement

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tui.editor.cursorUp` | `up` | Move cursor up |
| `tui.editor.cursorDown` | `down` | Move cursor down |
| `tui.editor.cursorLeft` | `left`, `ctrl+b` | Move cursor left |
| `tui.editor.cursorRight` | `right`, `ctrl+f` | Move cursor right |
| `tui.editor.cursorWordLeft` | `alt+left`, `ctrl+left`, `alt+b` | Move cursor word left |
| `tui.editor.cursorWordRight` | `alt+right`, `ctrl+right`, `alt+f` | Move cursor word right |
| `tui.editor.cursorLineStart` | `home`, `ctrl+a` | Move to line start |
| `tui.editor.cursorLineEnd` | `end`, `ctrl+e` | Move to line end |
| `tui.editor.jumpForward` | `ctrl+]` | Jump forward to character |
| `tui.editor.jumpBackward` | `ctrl+alt+]` | Jump backward to character |
| `tui.editor.pageUp` | `pageUp` | Scroll up by page |
| `tui.editor.pageDown` | `pageDown` | Scroll down by page |

### TUI Editor Deletion

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tui.editor.deleteCharBackward` | `backspace` | Delete character backward |
| `tui.editor.deleteCharForward` | `delete`, `ctrl+d` | Delete character forward |
| `tui.editor.deleteWordBackward` | `ctrl+w`, `alt+backspace` | Delete word backward |
| `tui.editor.deleteWordForward` | `alt+d`, `alt+delete` | Delete word forward |
| `tui.editor.deleteToLineStart` | `ctrl+u` | Delete to line start |
| `tui.editor.deleteToLineEnd` | `ctrl+k` | Delete to line end |

### TUI Input

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tui.input.newLine` | `shift+enter` | Insert new line |
| `tui.input.submit` | `enter` | Submit input |
| `tui.input.tab` | `tab` | Tab / autocomplete |

### TUI Kill Ring

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tui.editor.yank` | `ctrl+y` | Paste most recently deleted text |
| `tui.editor.yankPop` | `alt+y` | Cycle through deleted text after yank |
| `tui.editor.undo` | `ctrl+-` | Undo last edit |

### TUI Clipboard and Selection

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `tui.input.copy` | `ctrl+c` | Copy selection |
| `tui.select.up` | `up` | Move selection up |
| `tui.select.down` | `down` | Move selection down |
| `tui.select.pageUp` | `pageUp` | Page up in list |
| `tui.select.pageDown` | `pageDown` | Page down in list |
| `tui.select.confirm` | `enter` | Confirm selection |
| `tui.select.cancel` | `escape`, `ctrl+c` | Cancel selection |

### Application

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.interrupt` | `escape` | Cancel / abort |
| `app.clear` | `ctrl+c` | Clear editor |
| `app.exit` | `ctrl+d` | Exit (when editor empty) |
| `app.suspend` | `ctrl+z` (none on Windows) | Suspend to background |
| `app.editor.external` | `ctrl+g` | Open in external editor (`$VISUAL` or `$EDITOR`) |
| `app.clipboard.pasteImage` | `ctrl+v` (`alt+v` on Windows) | Paste image from clipboard |

### Sessions

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.session.new` | *(none)* | Start a new session (`/new`) |
| `app.session.tree` | *(none)* | Open session tree navigator (`/tree`) |
| `app.session.fork` | *(none)* | Fork current session (`/fork`) |
| `app.session.resume` | *(none)* | Open session resume picker (`/resume`) |
| `app.session.togglePath` | `ctrl+p` | Toggle path display |
| `app.session.toggleSort` | `ctrl+s` | Toggle sort mode |
| `app.session.toggleNamedFilter` | `ctrl+n` | Toggle named-only filter |
| `app.session.rename` | `ctrl+r` | Rename session |
| `app.session.delete` | `ctrl+d` | Delete session |
| `app.session.deleteNoninvasive` | `ctrl+backspace` | Delete session when query is empty |

### Models and Thinking

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.model.select` | `ctrl+l` | Open model selector |
| `app.model.cycleForward` | `ctrl+p` | Cycle to next model |
| `app.model.cycleBackward` | `shift+ctrl+p` | Cycle to previous model |
| `app.thinking.cycle` | `shift+tab` | Cycle thinking level |
| `app.thinking.toggle` | `ctrl+t` | Collapse or expand thinking blocks |

### Display and Message Queue

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.tools.expand` | `ctrl+o` | Collapse or expand tool output |
| `app.message.followUp` | `alt+enter` | Queue follow-up message |
| `app.message.dequeue` | `alt+up` | Restore queued messages to editor |

### Tree Navigation

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.tree.foldOrUp` | `ctrl+left`, `alt+left` | Fold current branch segment, or jump to the previous segment start |
| `app.tree.unfoldOrDown` | `ctrl+right`, `alt+right` | Unfold current branch segment, or jump to the next segment start or branch end |
| `app.tree.editLabel` | `shift+l` | Edit the label on the selected tree node |
| `app.tree.toggleLabelTimestamp` | `shift+t` | Toggle label timestamps in the tree |
| `app.tree.filter.default` | `ctrl+d` | Set tree filter to default view |
| `app.tree.filter.noTools` | `ctrl+t` | Toggle tree filter that hides tool results |
| `app.tree.filter.userOnly` | `ctrl+u` | Toggle tree filter that shows only user messages |
| `app.tree.filter.labeledOnly` | `ctrl+l` | Toggle tree filter that shows only labeled entries |
| `app.tree.filter.all` | `ctrl+a` | Toggle tree filter that shows all entries |
| `app.tree.filter.cycleForward` | `ctrl+o` | Cycle tree filter forward |
| `app.tree.filter.cycleBackward` | `shift+ctrl+o` | Cycle tree filter backward |

### Scoped Models Selector

Used inside the scoped models selector (opened via `/scoped-models`).

| Keybinding id | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `app.models.save` | `ctrl+s` | Save current model selection to settings |
| `app.models.enableAll` | `ctrl+a` | Enable all models (or all matching the current search) |
| `app.models.clearAll` | `ctrl+x` | Clear all models (or all matching the current search) |
| `app.models.toggleProvider` | `ctrl+p` | Toggle all models for the current provider |
| `app.models.reorderUp` | `alt+up` | Move the selected model up in the cycle order |
| `app.models.reorderDown` | `alt+down` | Move the selected model down in the cycle order |

## Custom Configuration

Create `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`:

```json
{
  "tui.editor.cursorUp": ["up", "ctrl+p"],
  "tui.editor.cursorDown": ["down", "ctrl+n"],
  "tui.editor.deleteWordBackward": ["ctrl+w", "alt+backspace"]
}
```

Each action can have a single key or an array of keys. User config overrides defaults.

On native Windows, `app.suspend` has no default binding because Windows terminals do not support Unix job control. If you bind it manually, pi shows a status message instead of suspending. In WSL, the normal Linux `ctrl+z`/`fg` behavior still applies.

### Emacs Example

```json
{
  "tui.editor.cursorUp": ["up", "ctrl+p"],
  "tui.editor.cursorDown": ["down", "ctrl+n"],
  "tui.editor.cursorLeft": ["left", "ctrl+b"],
  "tui.editor.cursorRight": ["right", "ctrl+f"],
  "tui.editor.cursorWordLeft": ["alt+left", "alt+b"],
  "tui.editor.cursorWordRight": ["alt+right", "alt+f"],
  "tui.editor.deleteCharForward": ["delete", "ctrl+d"],
  "tui.editor.deleteCharBackward": ["backspace", "ctrl+h"],
  "tui.input.newLine": ["shift+enter", "ctrl+j"]
}
```

### Vim Example

```json
{
  "tui.editor.cursorUp": ["up", "alt+k"],
  "tui.editor.cursorDown": ["down", "alt+j"],
  "tui.editor.cursorLeft": ["left", "alt+h"],
  "tui.editor.cursorRight": ["right", "alt+l"],
  "tui.editor.cursorWordLeft": ["alt+left", "alt+b"],
  "tui.editor.cursorWordRight": ["alt+right", "alt+w"]
}
```

content.text:
Press **`Escape`** to cancel/abort the agent mid-generation.

This is bound to the `app.interrupt` action by default. You can verify or change it in `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`.

content.text: The old lighthouse keeper climbed the spiral staircase for what he hoped would be the last time, his lantern casting long, dancing shadows against the damp stone walls. Below, the storm raged with a fury that had lasted three days, but now, as the first gray fingers of dawn crept across the horizon, the sea began to settle. He reached the top, unlocked the heavy shutters, and let the cool morning air rush in, carrying the scent of salt and possibility. Far out on the water, a ship's horn soundedвҖ”a single, deep note that seemed to say thank youвҖ”and the keeper smiled, knowing his watch was finally over.

- [Commands](#commands)
  - [Keyboard Shortcuts](#keyboard-shortcuts)
  - [Message Queue](#message-queue)
- [Sessions](#sessions)

--
| `/name <name>` | Set session display name |
| `/session` | Show session info (file, ID, messages, tokens, cost) |
| `/tree` | Jump to any point in the session and continue from there |
| `/fork` | Create a new session from a previous user message |
| `/clone` | Duplicate the current active branch into a new session |
| `/compact [prompt]` | Manually compact context, optional custom instructions |
| `/copy` | Copy last assistant message to clipboard |
| `/export [file]` | Export session to HTML file |
| `/share` | Upload as private GitHub gist with shareable HTML link |
| `/reload` | Reload keybindings, extensions, skills, prompts, and context files (themes hot-reload automatically) |
| `/hotkeys` | Show all keyboard shortcuts |
--

### Compaction

Long sessions can exhaust context windows. Compaction summarizes older messages while keeping recent ones.

**Manual:** `/compact` or `/compact <custom instructions>`

**Automatic:** Enabled by default. Triggers on context overflow (recovers and retries) or when approaching the limit (proactive). Configure via `/settings` or `settings.json`.

Compaction is lossy. The full history remains in the JSONL file; use `/tree` to revisit. Customize compaction behavior via [extensions](#extensions). See [docs/compaction.md](docs/compaction.md) for internals.

---

## Settings

--
The default export can also be `async`. pi waits for async extension factories before startup continues, which is useful for one-time initialization such as fetching remote model lists before calling `pi.registerProvider()`.

**What's possible:**
- Custom tools (or replace built-in tools entirely)
- Sub-agents and plan mode
- Custom compaction and summarization
- Permission gates and path protection
- Custom editors and UI components
- Status lines, headers, footers
- Git checkpointing and auto-commit
- SSH and sandbox execution

* @deprecated Use server-side compaction instead by passing
 * `edits: [{ type: 'compact_20260112' }]` in the params passed to `toolRunner()`.
 * See https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction
   * The context token threshold at which to trigger compaction.
   * The model to use for generating the compaction summary.

content.text:
Here's how compaction works and how to adjust it:

## How Compaction Works

When your conversation grows too long, pi summarizes older messages to free up context tokens. It does this in two scenarios:

1. **Automatic** вҖ” triggers when `contextTokens > contextWindow - reserveTokens` (i.e., when you're running low on context)
2. **Manual** вҖ” triggered with `/compact`

The process:
1. Keeps your most recent `keepRecentTokens` worth of messages intact
2. Summarizes everything before that into a structured summary (goal, progress, decisions, file ops, etc.)
3. Replaces the old messages with a single `CompactionEntry` containing the summary

## How to Make It Less Aggressive

In `~/.pi/agent/settings.json`, you can adjust two settings:

```json
{
  "compaction": {
    "enabled": true,
    "reserveTokens": 24576,
    "keepRecentTokens": 30000
  }
}
```

| Setting | Default | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| `reserveTokens` | `16384` | Tokens reserved for the LLM's response. **Increasing this** means compaction triggers sooner (less room before context is full). |
| `keepRecentTokens` | `20000` | Recent tokens kept uncompressed. **Increasing this** means more messages stay in full form before being summarized. |

**To make compaction less aggressive**, increase `keepRecentTokens` so more recent messages are preserved. For example, `30000` would keep roughly 1.5x more recent history uncompressed.

**To disable compaction entirely**, set `"enabled": false` вҖ” though this will cause issues when you actually hit the context limit (you'll get context overflow errors).

Note: Compaction is lossy. Full history is still saved in the session file; you can use `/tree` to navigate back to any point in the conversation.

What about when using a planning extention such as plannotator? I want the plan to still be preserve perfectly, but what if we are already done with setep 1 of 6 and I only have like 20% of my context left? I don't really care about the exact details of what I just did, but I care a ton about the plan that might be pretty far back in the context

content.thinking:
This is a really good question about how compaction interacts with plans that might be buried in older context. The user's concern is: when compaction kicks in and summarizes old messages, it might lose the plan details that are critical for continuing work.

Let me think about what I know from the compaction docs:

1. Compaction generates a structured summary with sections like "Goal", "Progress", "Next Steps", "Critical Context"
2. It tracks file operations cumulatively
3. The summary format includes "Goal", "Constraints & Preferences", "Progress" (Done/In Progress/Blocked), "Key Decisions", "Next Steps", "Critical Context"

So the compaction summary format is actually designed to preserve high-level plan information! The structured summary format explicitly captures:


- Goal
- Progress (Done, In Progress, Blocked)
- Next Steps
- Key Decisions
- Critical Context

But the user is right to worry - the summary is a compressed representation and could lose details about their planning extension. I should look into how plannotator works and whether there are extension hooks to customize compaction, particularly the `session_before_compact` hook that lets me provide a custom summary or cancel it entirely. I need to understand what information plannotator preserves and whether the default compaction is handling it well.

content.text:
That's a great concern. The good news is the compaction summary format is actually designed for this вҖ” it explicitly captures:

- **Goal** вҖ” what you're trying to accomplish
- **Progress** (Done, In Progress, Blocked)
- **Next Steps** вҖ” what should happen next
- **Key Decisions** вҖ” with rationale
- **Critical Context** вҖ” data needed to continue

So your plan steps should survive compaction in theory. But there's a catch: the summary is compressed by an LLM, and with your current model (`qwen3.6:35b-llama.cpp`), detail fidelity depends heavily on the model's quality. A smaller model can easily drop nuance.

## The real solution: extend compaction for plan-aware summaries

You can customize compaction via extension hooks. Here's the approach:

content.thinking: Let me look at the index.ts to understand how plannotator stores plan state and whether there's any compaction-related logic.

This is a really insightful question. Let me think about how plannotator plans survive compaction and what the user can do to ensure their plan stays preserved.

Key observations from the code:

1. **The plan file is on disk** - plannotator reads the plan from a markdown file on disk (`lastSubmittedPath`), not just from conversation context. The `before_agent_start` hook re-reads it from disk every turn. This is a critical insight.

2. **The plan is also persisted as a custom entry** in the session via `pi.appendEntry("plannotator", { phase, lastSubmittedPath, savedState })` and `pi.appendEntry("plannotator-execute", { lastSubmittedPath })`.
   
3. **When executing**, the system injects the remaining steps as a custom message at the start of each turn, so even if older context gets compacted away, the plan gets re-injected from the current checklist state.

So the plan survives compaction in multiple ways: it's stored on disk as a file that gets re-read each turn, and the remaining steps are continuously re-injected as system context during execution. The main risk is if the plan file gets deleted or modified outside the session, but the execution checklist provides a secondary safeguard.

To make compaction even more reliable for plannotator, I could suggest increasing `keepRecentTokens` to preserve more recent conversation history, or creating a custom extension hook that ensures the plan state is explicitly included in compaction summaries when they happen. I'm realizing there's actually a tension hereвҖ”since plannotator re-reads the plan from disk and injects the remaining steps via hooks, keeping the full conversation with the user's annotations around those steps might actually be more valuable than having the plan itself in the compacted context. Let me work out the best approach for the user.

content.text:
Great question вҖ” and the answer is actually pretty reassuring. Here's why:

## Plannotator is already resilient to compaction

Plannotator survives compaction in **three layers**:

### 1. The plan lives on disk, not in context
Your plan markdown file is read from disk every turn by the `before_agent_start` hook. Even if compaction wipes the plan from the conversation context entirely, the agent can re-read it:

```typescript
// In before_agent_start вҖ” re-reads from disk every turn
const planContent = readFileSync(fullPath, "utf-8");
checklistItems = parseChecklist(planContent);
```

### 2. Remaining steps are re-injected
When executing, plannotator injects the remaining unchecked steps into every agent turn:

```
[PLANNOTATOR - EXECUTING PLAN]
Full tool access is enabled. Execute the plan from PLAN.md

Remaining steps:
- [ ] Step 4: Refactor the auth module
- [ ] Step 5: Add integration tests
- [ ] Step 6: Update documentation
```

So after compaction, the agent knows exactly where it left off.

### 3. Plan file is persisted as a session entry
The path is saved as a custom `plannotator` entry in the session JSONL, surviving session resume.

## Where you CAN lose things

What compaction CAN lose is the **user annotations and feedback** on plan steps. If you annotated specific steps with feedback like "this approach is wrong, use X instead," that feedback lives in the conversation messages being compacted. The agent might forget *why* a step was modified.

## What to do about it

**Option A: Increase `keepRecentTokens` (quick fix)**

```json
{
  "compaction": {
    "enabled": true,
    "reserveTokens": 16384,
    "keepRecentTokens": 40000
  }
}
```

This doubles the recent context preserved, meaning annotations and feedback on earlier steps survive longer.

**Option B: Add plan annotations to the plan file itself (robust)**

When you annotate or give feedback on steps, ask the agent to update the plan file in place with your feedback:

> "Please update PLAN.md to include my feedback about step 2 вҖ” write it as a comment in the markdown so the agent context won't lose it."

This way, the feedback survives even if compaction is aggressive, because it's on disk alongside the plan.

**Option C: Custom compaction hook (advanced)**

You could write an extension that intercepts `session_before_compact` and ensures the plan file content + remaining checklist is always included in the compaction summary's "Critical Context":

```typescript
pi.on("session_before_compact", async (event, ctx) => {
  if (phase !== "executing" || !lastSubmittedPath) return;

  const fullPath = resolve(ctx.cwd, lastSubmittedPath);
  const planContent = readFileSync(fullPath, "utf-8");
  const remaining = checklistItems.filter((t) => !t.completed);

  event.compact!.customInstructions = `
IMPORTANT: Preserve the plan and remaining steps in the summary.

PLAN:
${planContent}

REMAINING STEPS:
${remaining.map(t => `- [ ] ${t.step}. ${t.text}`).join("\n")}

Do not summarize away the plan structure or remaining steps.
`;
});
```

## My recommendation

Start with **Option A** (increase `keepRecentTokens`). If you're doing heavy annotation-based planning, also adopt **Option B** вҖ” get in the habit of having the agent write your feedback directly into the plan file. That's the bulletproof approach: on-disk state never gets lost to compaction.

```
/compact
/session           # shows new state
```

This won't show you *what* changed, but gives you before/after token counts and message counts.

## Practical options (in order of ease):

### 1. Check the compaction summary inline
When compaction runs, it **displays the summary to you** right before it happens. You can see exactly what got summarized and what was kept. This is the built-in way.

### 2. Compare the compaction summary before/after
If you run `/compact` manually, you get to see the summary it generates. After compaction, run `/compact` again вҖ” you'll see the *new* summary that replaces the old one. Comparing the two compaction entries in the session JSONL shows you the evolution:

```bash
# After compaction, view the session file:
cat ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<session-id>.jsonl | jq '.[] | select(.type == "compaction") | .summary'
```

### 3. Use `/session` for token counts
```
/session           # note token count
/compact
/session           # compare token count
```

content.text:
Hmm, that extension is getting complicated and depends on internal types that might not be exported. Let me give you a much simpler answer:

## The simplest approach: Compaction already shows you the summary

When compaction runs (either automatically or via `/compact`), **pi displays the generated summary to you right before it applies it**. This IS the diff вҖ” it shows you exactly what was compressed into a summary and what was kept.

So:

1. Run `/compact` вҖ” pi shows you the summary it's about to generate
2. It applies the compaction and appends the `CompactionEntry`
3. The summary tells you: tokens replaced, what was kept, what was summarized

## If you want to compare multiple compactons

After a couple of compactons, the session JSONL has the compaction entries. You can extract them:

```bash
# List all compaction entries in the session
cat ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<session-file>.jsonl | python3 -c "
import sys, json
for line in sys.stdin:
    entry = json.loads(line)
    if entry.get('type') == 'compaction':
        print(f\"--- Compaction #{entry['id']} ---\")
        print(f\"Tokens replaced: {entry['tokensBefore']}\")
        print(f\"Keeps from: {entry['firstKeptEntryId']}\")
        print(entry['summary'])
        print()
"
```

## If you want a dedicated diff command

1. Compaction replaces old messages with a summary
2. The summary is shown when compaction runs
3. The remaining messages are kept in context

The simplest approach for the user is:

1. **Before compaction**: The full message history is in context
2. **After compaction**: The compaction summary replaces old messages, kept messages are still there

To see the actual difference, the user could:
- Look at the session JSONL before/after


- Use the extension I wrote to dump compaction summaries
- Simply watch what pi displays when compaction runs

I should give them a clear, actionable answer: the summary pi shows you during compaction IS the diff, and if they need to compare across multiple runs, the extension will help. The user is asking what actually changes in the context window when compaction runs. Looking at the compaction docs, I can see the mechanics: it removes messages before a certain point, keeps recent ones, and adds a summary entry. So I should explain this clearly with concrete examples showing what the context looks like before and after, rather than overcomplicating it with extensions or tools.

content.text:
Actually, let me step back. The simplest answer is right there in how compaction works вҖ” here's exactly what happens:

## What changes in context after compaction

**Before compaction**, the LLM sees:
```
system prompt вҶ’ message 1 вҶ’ tool 1 вҶ’ message 2 вҶ’ tool 2 вҶ’ ... вҶ’ message N вҶ’ tool N
```

**After compaction**, the LLM sees:
```
system prompt вҶ’ [SUMMARY of messages 1..M] вҶ’ message M+1 вҶ’ tool M+1 вҶ’ ... вҶ’ message N вҶ’ tool N
```

The `SUMMARY` is displayed to you when compaction runs вҖ” that's exactly what replaced. The messages from `M+1` onward are untouched.

## To see this concretely:

1. **Run `/session` before compaction** вҖ” note the message count and token estimate
2. **Run `/compact`** вҖ” pi shows you the summary it generated (this is what replaced the old messages)
3. **Run `/session` after** вҖ” compare the token count

The token drop tells you how much was compressed. The summary text tells you what was preserved.

## If you want to dig deeper

The extension I wrote at `~/.pi/agent/extensions/context-diff.ts` gives you:

- **`/compact-show`** вҖ” prints all compaction summaries from the session
- **`/compact-diff`** вҖ” writes them to numbered files for `diff -u`

Just run `/reload` after adding it to make it available.

**Bottom line**: Compaction already shows you the diff вҖ” it displays the summary it's about to insert. If you want to compare across multiple compactons, you need to capture them (via the extension or manually).

## Constraints & Preferences

## Key Decisions
- **TUI Interrupt Action**: Bound to `app.interrupt` with default key `Escape`. Can be customized in `~/.pi/agent/keybindings.json`.

## Next Steps
1. Complete the response to the current "test" message.
2. Await further user instructions or development tasks.

## Critical Context
- Environment: Running inside pi agent harness at `/home/tychart/.pi/agent`
- Documentation reviewed: `keybindings.md`, `tui.md` (path: `/home/tychart/.nvm/versions/node/v26.1.0/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/`)
- Node version: v26.1.0 via nvm
- Tools available: read, bash, edit, write, and TUI rendering capabilities

---

**Turn Context (split turn):**

## Original Request
User asked how the "compaction" feature in the pi agent works and how to configure it to be less aggressive.

## Early Progress
- Searched the `pi-coding-agent` directory for documentation and source code related to compaction.
- Located `docs/compaction.md` and `README.md`, which explain that compaction automatically summarizes older messages when the conversation approaches the context window limit, freeing tokens for new interactions.
- Identified the auto-compaction trigger condition: `contextTokens > contextWindow - reserveTokens`.
- Noted that compaction can be triggered manually via `/compact`, is lossy (though full history remains in the JSONL file), and can be customized via settings or extensions.

## Context for Suffix
- The assistant is mid-explanation of the compaction trigger logic and is about to detail how to adjust `reserveTokens` or related token thresholds via `settings.json` or the `/settings` command to reduce compaction frequency.
- Suffix requires knowledge of the exact configuration parameters in `docs/compaction.md`, how the pi agent reads them, and step-by-step instructions for lowering the aggressiveness.
